title: Why Do Titles Change How People Treat Me?

situation.content:
You got the promotion. Or the title change. Or the corner office. Nothing about you changed — same skills, same personality, same ideas. But something shifted in how people relate to you. They laugh harder at your jokes. They agree faster with your proposals. They share less honest feedback. The title changed nothing about you and everything about the social physics around you.

Or the reverse: you lost the title. Same person, same brain. But suddenly your calls aren't returned as quickly, your opinions carry less weight, your presence in the room feels different. What changed? Nothing real. Everything social.

story.content:
In 1961, Stanley Milgram placed an ad in a New Haven newspaper: "WE WILL PAY YOU $4.50 FOR ONE HOUR OF YOUR TIME." Ordinary people showed up — teachers, clerks, salesmen, laborers. They were told they'd be participating in a study on learning and memory.

The setup: a "learner" (actually an actor) was strapped to a chair in another room. The participant sat at a console with voltage switches ranging from 15 to 450 volts. Labels read: "Slight Shock," "Moderate Shock," "Danger: Severe Shock," and finally "XXX." Each time the learner gave a wrong answer, the participant was told to increase the voltage.

At 150 volts, the learner screamed and demanded to be released. At 300 volts, he pounded the wall and went silent. The experimenter — a man in a grey lab coat — simply said: "The experiment requires that you continue." Or: "You have no other choice, you must go on."

65% of participants went all the way to 450 volts. Not sadists. Not psychopaths. Ordinary people — because a man in a lab coat with the title of "scientist" told them to.

Milgram's insight wasn't that people are evil. It was that authority — even minimal, performative authority like a lab coat and a clipboard — restructures human behavior at a fundamental level. The participants weren't obeying because they were threatened. They were obeying because the presence of an authority figure shifted the cognitive framework from "I am responsible for my choices" to "I am executing instructions from someone qualified to decide."

The title didn't give the experimenter actual expertise in ethics or the right to harm people. It gave him perceived legitimacy — and perceived legitimacy is all authority needs to function. The lab coat wasn't power. It was the costume of power. And it was sufficient.

This is why titles change everything: not because they grant actual ability, but because they activate an obedience script that evolved in hierarchical primate groups over millions of years. The brain has a module for detecting authority and a default behavior when authority is detected: defer. This module doesn't check whether the authority is legitimate or whether the instruction is ethical. It fires on cue — on title, on uniform, on confidence of delivery.

In organizations, this manifests as: the VP's idea getting less scrutiny than the intern's. The titled person's email getting faster responses. The manager's joke landing harder. None of this reflects the quality of the idea, the urgency of the email, or the humor of the joke. It reflects the authority bias — the systematic distortion of perception that titles produce in everyone around the titled person.
story.title: The Story

contrast.content:
In the Mahabharata, Eklavya was a tribal boy who wanted to learn archery from Drona, the royal teacher. Drona refused — Eklavya was not of the right caste. So Eklavya built a clay statue of Drona and practiced alone, becoming the greatest archer in the land.

When Drona discovered this, he demanded Eklavya's right thumb as "guru dakshina" (teacher's fee) — knowing it would destroy his archery forever. Eklavya cut off his thumb without hesitation.

This is the dark side of authority's power: Eklavya surrendered his greatest ability not because Drona deserved it, but because the title of "guru" activated an obedience deeper than self-interest. The authority wasn't earned (Drona never taught him). The deference was voluntary. The cost was permanent. Titles don't just distort perception — they can make people complicit in their own diminishment.
contrast.title: The Contrast

principle.content:
Titles don't grant ability. They activate an obedience script in others that distorts perception, reduces scrutiny, and generates unearned deference. This isn't a flaw in other people — it's a feature of hierarchical primate cognition that evolved for survival.

If you hold a title: everything around you is now distorted. People agree faster, challenge less, and filter what they tell you. If you don't hold one: your ideas face more scrutiny than identical ideas from titled people. Neither situation reflects reality. Both are the authority bias at work.

psychology.content:
The authority bias operates through a specific cognitive mechanism called "agency shift" — the psychological state where a person redefines themselves from "autonomous agent" to "instrument of authority." In this state:

1. **Responsibility transfers**: "I'm not deciding. I'm following instructions." The person experiences reduced moral weight because they perceive the authority as the decision-maker.

2. **Critical thinking reduces**: When an authority figure states something, the brain's default is acceptance rather than evaluation. The prefrontal cortex (critical reasoning) shows reduced activation in the presence of perceived expertise.

3. **Social proof compounds it**: In Milgram's experiments, when other participants (confederates) refused to continue, obedience dropped to 10%. But when others complied, individual resistance collapsed. Authority + social proof is nearly irresistible.

Research shows that the authority bias is modulated by three factors: perceived legitimacy ("Is this person qualified to direct me?"), proximity ("Can the authority see whether I comply?"), and personal responsibility ("Will I be accountable for what happens?"). Milgram found that moving the authority figure to a telephone reduced obedience from 65% to 21%. Distance weakens the spell.

The implication for titled people: your distance from the team is proportional to how distorted the information you receive will be. The more authority you accumulate, the harder you must work to receive accurate feedback.
psychology.title: Why It Happens

move.content:
1. If you hold a title: actively counter the distortion field. Your title makes people agree faster and challenge less. This means your bad ideas face less scrutiny than they should. Deliberately create channels for dissent: anonymous feedback, designated devil's advocates, speaking last in meetings. The authority bias won't disappear, but you can build structures that partially compensate.

2. If you don't hold a title: name the dynamic without accusation. "I notice that proposal got less pushback than mine did — could be quality, could be title weight. Can we evaluate both on merits?" Naming the bias doesn't eliminate it, but it creates a moment of awareness that can shift behavior.

3. Separate the person from the title when evaluating ideas. Before reacting to a proposal, ask: "Would I respond the same way if this idea came from the newest person on the team?" If the answer is no, you're responding to authority, not quality.

4. Watch for Eklavya moments. When someone with a title asks you for something that costs you disproportionately — a sacrifice of credit, time, opportunity, or capability — notice whether you're complying because it's right or because the title activated automatic deference. Authority doesn't equal legitimacy.

5. Use your title deliberately, not passively. If you have authority, deploy it intentionally rather than letting it radiate indiscriminately. Use your title to amplify others ("I want to hear what Maya thinks"), to create safety ("I was wrong about this last time"), and to reduce the distortion rather than exploit it.
move.title: The Move
